title: Leaf-Shaped Nesting Tables
summary: A set of three graduated leaf-shaped side tables cut from oak plywood on purchased furniture legs.
description:
Nesting tables usually come as a matched rectangular set; these three lean into an organic leaf shape instead, which reads as sculptural rather than purely functional while still stacking together the same way a traditional nesting set does.

Each top is cut from its own **hand-drawn paper template**, sized to graduate from largest to smallest so the set nests cleanly — there's no fixed numeric cut list here on purpose, since the shape and scale are meant to fit your own space rather than a standard dimension.

**Purchased furniture legs**, not shop-built ones, are the right call here — a turned or tapered leg that matches the tops' organic shape is a specialized turning job, and buying legs lets the build focus on the tabletop shaping instead.
timeLabel: 2.5–4 hrs for the set of three

tools.note: Trues up long straight edges after the curved cuts.

steps.title: Make the templates
steps.body: Draw and cut three leaf-shaped templates from craft paper, graduated largest to smallest and sized to the room they'll live in rather than to any standard dimension — there is no fixed cut list here on purpose. Work out the step-down between sizes by laying the paper shapes on top of each other and sliding them around, not by picking a number: the two smaller leaves have to tuck under the largest without their edges or their legs colliding, and that is a geometry you can only see by overlapping the templates. Keep the stem end of each leaf reasonably wide — a fine point is where a plywood top is weakest and where edge banding is hardest to wrap. Once all three nest cleanly, cut the paper cleanly and label them, because the templates are the only record of the sizes you chose.

steps.title: Trace and cut the tops
steps.body: Lay each template on the 3/4" oak plywood and orient it so the face grain runs along the leaf's long axis — in a shape with no straight edges, grain direction is doing all the visual work, and a top cut across the grain reads as an offcut. Trace with a pencil, then cut just outside the line with the jigsaw so there is material left to sand back to; use a fine, down-cutting or scrolling blade so the oak veneer doesn't chip out on the show face, and let the saw follow the curve rather than forcing it around. Where the outline includes a long straight run, true it with the circular saw against a straightedge — a jigsaw wanders over a long cut, and a wavy straight edge reads as a mistake where a wavy curve doesn't. Sand each top back to the line and fair the curve by eye before moving on, because every dip left here will telegraph through the edge banding.

steps.title: Band the edges
steps.body: Apply the edge banding to every cut edge, working around the curves a short section at a time and pressing each section down as it cools so it can't lift on the outside of a tight bend. Trim the overhang flush with a banding trimmer or a sharp chisel held flat against the face, then sand the joint lightly — sand ALONG the edge rather than across it, because cutting through the thin banding into the veneer beneath is easy and effectively unfixable. This is the step that makes a plywood top read as a solid slab; an exposed ply edge on a curve is the giveaway that gives the whole set away.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Turn each top face down on a padded surface and lay out the leg positions before drilling anything, keeping them far enough in from the edge that no leg shows past the leaf's outline when the table is viewed from above, and far enough apart that setting a mug on the overhang doesn't tip it. Fasten through the mounting plate or block with pocket screws and glue, checking the screw length can't break through the 3/4" top — on a leaf shape you may be screwing near a thinning section where there is less room for error than you'd expect. Stand each finished table on a known-flat surface and confirm it doesn't rock before the glue cures. Then set all three together and check they still nest: a leg an inch out of position is exactly what stops a nesting set from nesting.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand all three tops through the grits and ease the top and bottom edges slightly, so the edge banding can't be caught by a sleeve and peeled. Finish all three at the same time, in the same order, from the same can — these tables are seen as a set, so a colour mismatch between them is far more obvious than any imperfection in a single one. Finish the undersides too: an unfinished underside on a plywood top lets the two faces take on moisture at different rates, which is what makes a flat top stop being flat.
